name: scope-statement
title: Define the MVP scope
description: The foundational scoping decision. Records what the MVP must ship, what it explicitly won't, and how success is measured. Usually the first DR a project creates.

starter.summary: Explicit MVP scope boundaries — what ships, what doesn't, and what 'done' means.
starter.issue: Without explicit scope, work expands silently until the project never ships. Pin the MVP perimeter so the team can disagree-and-commit and so the decomposing phase has a clear target.
assumptions: The team has a shared rough idea of what the project is.
Stakeholders are reachable for scope disputes.

positions.title: Lean MVP (smallest thing that proves the core hypothesis)
positions.description: Ship the minimum slice that lets us learn whether the idea is right.
cons: Might miss the 'aha' moment if too thin

positions.title: Walking-skeleton MVP (end-to-end thin slice across all layers)
positions.description: All systems touched, but each only at minimum depth.
cons: Still misses depth users may need

positions.title: Polished MVP (feature-thin but UX-polished)
positions.description: Few features but they feel finished.
cons: Polish on wrong features is waste

implications: Defines the perimeter of acceptable feature work in the deciding and decomposing phases.
Out-of-scope items become explicit Future Work artifacts.
Success criteria gate the eventual launch decision.

notes_for_agent: This seed should be the FIRST decision instantiated in nearly every project. Customize the issue with the actual project description. The selected position is usually 'walking-skeleton MVP' for technical projects with novel architecture; 'lean MVP' for hypothesis-driven product bets; 'polished MVP' for projects in crowded markets where first impression matters.
